blocks.text: A polynomial in one variable x is an expression of the form a₀ + a₁x + a₂x² + ... + aₙxⁿ, where a₀, a₁, a₂,... are real numbers and n is a non-negative integer.
The highest power of x in a polynomial is called its degree. Based on degree, polynomials can be classified as linear (degree 1), quadratic (degree 2), cubic (degree 3), etc.

topics.title: Geometrical Meaning of Zeroes of a Polynomial

blocks.text: The zeroes of a polynomial are the x-coordinates of the points where the graph of the polynomial intersects the x-axis.
Consider the linear polynomial y = 2x + 3. To draw its graph, we first prepare a table of values.
From the graph, we observe that the line intersects the x-axis at x = -3/2. Therefore, the zero of the polynomial 2x + 3 is -3/2.

blocks.description: Sum of product of zeroes taken two at a time

blocks.label: Example 1
blocks.question: Find the quadratic polynomial whose zeroes are 2 and −3.
blocks.solution: Sum = 2 + (−3) = −1. Product = 2 × (−3) = −6. Required polynomial = x² − (sum)x + product = x² + x − 6.

blocks.text: For any two polynomials p(x) and g(x), where g(x) ≠ 0, there exist unique polynomials q(x) and r(x) such that:
The degree of remainder r(x) is always less than the degree of g(x).

blocks.label: Example 2
blocks.question: Divide 3x³ − 5x² − 4x + 4 by x − 2.
blocks.solution: Using long division, quotient = 3x² + x − 2 and remainder = 0.

questions: The graph of y = p(x) is given. Find the number of zeroes of p(x).
Find the zeroes of the polynomial x² − 2.
Find the quadratic polynomial whose zeroes are 2 and −3.
Find a cubic polynomial whose zeroes are 1, −1 and 2.
Verify the relationship between zeroes and coefficients for x² − 7x + 10.
Verify division algorithm for p(x) = x³ − 3x² + 3x − 1 and g(x) = x − 1.

blocks.text: A polynomial has degree equal to the highest power of x. The zeroes of a polynomial are the x-coordinates where its graph intersects the x-axis. The relationship between zeroes and coefficients helps in forming polynomials. The division algorithm states that p(x) = g(x)q(x) + r(x), where the degree of r(x) is less than the degree of g(x).
